title: Chest of Drawers from 2x4s
summary: A solid four-drawer dresser built almost entirely from construction-grade 2x4s and pocket screws. Cheap lumber, real furniture.
description:
This is the trick every 2x4-furniture build turns on: cheap softwood becomes real furniture the moment the joinery is hidden and the faces are flat. The whole case here is 2x4 rails and legs joined with pocket screws, with the pocket holes deliberately placed where the top and the drawer fronts will cover them.

The one number that will bite you is the actual width of a 2x4. Nominal is 3.5", but a given board can run a hair under, and the side panels are built from four 2x4s edge to edge — small differences stack up. Build the side infill panels first, measure what you actually got, and trim the 14" cross rails to match. Don't cut every rail to the drawing and hope.

Drawers ride on standard side-mount slides, so the openings have to be square and consistent. The false-front trick — screwing a finished drawer front onto the plain drawer box from the inside — is what lets you dial in even reveals at the end instead of praying the boxes came out perfect. Build the boxes to fit the slides, then float the fronts.
timeLabel: 10–15 hrs over a weekend, plus finishing time

tools.note: The entire case is pocket-screwed. This build assumes you have one.
A lot of repeated square crosscuts — a stop block keeps the rails identical.
Construction 2x4s are rough — sanding is what separates this from looking like a pallet.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, rails, and the side infill panels. Pick the straightest, driest boards you can find.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: The four boards for the top.

materials.name: 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Small drawer front and drawer box sides/backs.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood
materials.note: Drawer bottoms and the back panels. A half sheet is plenty.

materials.name: Side-mount drawer slides
materials.note: Buy the slides first — the drawer boxes are built to fit them, not the other way around.

materials.name: Wood filler, pre-stain conditioner, and finish
materials.note: The pictured piece used a pre-stain conditioner, a stain, and a matte poly.

cutList.note: Trim to fit once the side infill panels are built — actual 2x4 width varies.
Fill each side between the top and bottom rails. Pocket holes on the inside face. Or substitute a plywood panel for a flat-panel look.
Pocket holes hidden top and bottom. Cut to fit your actual carcass width.
Glued up edge to edge; the length gives a ~3/4" overhang each end.
1/4" narrower than the opening; measure your real opening and cut to fit.
1/4" narrower than the opening; measure and cut to fit.
Sized with ~1" overlap onto the frame all around.

steps.title: Build the two side panels
steps.body:
Make each side infill from four 2x4s laid face to face, with pocket holes drilled on the inside so they disappear once assembled. Drill a pocket hole into the top and bottom of each infill panel too — that's how the panel ties into the top and bottom rails.

If you'd rather have a smooth, flat side, drop in a plywood panel here instead. Build both sides identical before you go further.

steps.title: Frame each side with legs and rails
steps.body:
Attach a 29.5" leg to each end of a side panel, then cap the panel with the 14" top and bottom rails.

Here's the gotcha: a 2x4 is rarely exactly 3.5" wide, and four of them glued up rarely add to exactly what the drawing says. Measure the panel you actually built and trim the 14" rails to suit. Force the drawing onto reality and the case ends up out of square.

steps.title: Add the back rails
steps.body:
Join the two side assemblies with the back rails, pocket holes facing in so they're hidden from behind. Drill the pockets on the narrow (1.5") face of each rail.

Check the case for square across both diagonals before you move to the front — everything downstream depends on this box being true.

steps.title: Add the front rails
steps.body:
Install the front rails to define the drawer openings. Put the top rail's pocket holes on top (hidden by the top) and the bottom rail's underneath. Drill the pockets on the narrow end of each piece.

The openings these rails create are what the drawers fit into, so keep them even side to side.

steps.title: Build the drawer boxes
steps.body:
Drill pocket holes in both ends of the 15-1/2" drawer front/back pieces, then join them to the 16" sides to form each box. Either rout a groove for a 1/4" plywood bottom or simply fasten the bottom to the underside of the box.

Build the boxes to the width your slides need, not to a nominal number — most side-mount slides want exactly 1" total clearance (1/2" per side).

steps.title: Mount the slides
steps.body:
Follow the instructions that came with your slides to mount them to the drawer boxes and the case. As a rule you'll want about a 1/2" gap between the bottom of a drawer and the rail beneath it, but the slide instructions are the authority here.

Test each drawer for smooth travel before adding fronts.

steps.title: Float the drawer fronts
steps.body:
Fit the false fronts to the openings with an even reveal at the top and outer edges — the small front is the 1x6 at ~17-3/4", the large fronts are the 1x8s. Measure your real openings and cut to fit; don't trust the nominal number.

Drill two holes through the drawer box from inside, position the false front exactly where you want it, and screw out through the box into the front with 1-1/4" screws. Floating the fronts this way is what gives you dead-even gaps even if a box is slightly off.

steps.title: Glue up and attach the top
steps.body:
Edge-glue the four 2x6s into a top ~22.5" long, so it overhangs about 3/4" on each side.

Drill pocket holes on the side and back rails facing up, and drill down through the front rail. Lay the finished top face-down, set the upturned case on it with an even overhang all around, then fix the sides and back with 1-1/4" pocket screws and the front edge with 2-1/2" screws up through the front rail.

steps.title: Fit the back panels
steps.body: Drop the 1/4" back panels in from inside, leaving at least a 1/2" gap around the edge, and pin them with 3/4" finishing nails. The panels are sized to overlap the frame about 1" all around.

steps.title: Sand, finish, and add hardware
steps.body:
Sand everything well to knock down the joints and clean off glue squeeze-out — construction lumber needs this more than nice hardwood does. Apply a pre-stain conditioner (pine blotches badly without it), then your stain or paint and a protective topcoat.

Temporarily pull the drawer fronts, drill for your pulls, and reinstall. Done.
materials: Wood filler, pre-stain conditioner, and finish

images.alt: A four-drawer chest of drawers built from construction 2x4s with a stained finish
